title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a sensory branding expert called Hollie Bennett talking about the use of scent in commercial environments. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a sensory branding expert called Hollie Bennett talking about the use of scent in commercial environments. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Hollie Bennett explores how businesses use fragrances to influence customer behavior and emotions.
extracts.audio_script:
Hi, I’m Hollie. When you walk into a hotel or a high-end store, you might notice a pleasant smell. It’s not an accident; it’s a carefully engineered part of the brand’s identity. I’m a sensory branding consultant. My interest in the power of smell didn't start with a perfume bottle; it actually began when I read a [7]scientific paper[/7] about how scents are processed in the limbic system, the part of the brain that controls emotions. I was fascinated by the idea that a smell can trigger a memory more effectively than a sound or an image.

Scent marketing is about creating an emotional connection. I’ve found that the [8]scent intensity[/8] is the most important factor in whether a campaign is successful. If it’s too strong, it becomes irritating; if it’s too subtle, it’s ignored. We use specialized 'nebulizers' to disperse the fragrance through the air conditioning system, ensuring a consistent experience throughout the building.

Different industries use different types of scents. For example, in supermarkets, the smell of freshly baked bread is used to increase appetite and encourage impulse buys. I noticed that [9]citrus notes[/9] are often used in gyms and offices because they are associated with cleanliness and energy. They can actually improve focus and productivity among employees. It’s a subtle way to enhance the working environment.

In the hospitality sector, brands are now creating their own 'signature scents'. I recently worked with a hotel chain that wanted a fragrance that felt both luxurious and welcoming. I found that [10]sandalwood and vanilla[/10] were the perfect combination for achieving this. Now, when a guest walks into any of their hotels worldwide, they immediately feel a sense of familiarity and comfort. It builds incredible brand loyalty.

However, we have to be careful about cultural differences. I’ve found that a scent that is perceived as pleasant in Europe might be seen as [11]medicinal or offensive[/11] in parts of Asia. For instance, lavender is often associated with relaxation in the West, but in some other cultures, it’s linked to cleaning products or old age. You have to do extensive research before launching a global scent campaign.

Technology is allowing us to be more precise. I’ve noticed that [12]proximity sensors[/12] are now being used to release a burst of fragrance only when a customer approaches a specific display. This prevents 'scent fatigue', where the customer stops noticing the smell after a few minutes. It keeps the sensory experience fresh and engaging.

Looking forward, I’m interested in the use of scent in virtual reality. I believe that [13]digital scent devices[/13] will eventually be a standard part of home entertainment systems. Imagine watching a cooking show and being able to smell the ingredients as they are being prepared. It would take immersion to a completely new level.

Finally, I’m currently finishing a [14]market report[/14] on the ethics of subconscious marketing. I want to start a conversation about how much influence brands should be allowed to have over our senses. Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: hollie

questions.question: Hollie’s interest in smell began after she encountered a (7) during her studies.
questions.explanation: She mentions it 'began when I read a scientific paper about how scents are processed'.

questions.question: Hollie explains that the (8) is the most vital aspect to get right in a scent campaign.
questions.explanation: She has found that 'the scent intensity is the most important factor'.

questions.question: Hollie notes that (9) are commonly used to create an energetic atmosphere in professional settings.
questions.explanation: She noticed that 'citrus notes are often used in gyms and offices'.

questions.explanation: She found that 'sandalwood and vanilla were the perfect combination'.

questions.question: Hollie warns that some scents may be viewed as (11) depending on the country.
questions.explanation: She's found that a scent pleasant in Europe might be seen as 'medicinal or offensive' elsewhere.
